summary: Gruber analyzes Six Apart's botched Movable Type 3.0 launch, which angered even loyal paying users. He identifies two critical mistakes: releasing a paid upgrade with almost no new features, and pre-announcing it as a 'free upgrade' months earlier only to reverse course on pricing. He argues that people pay for features, not engine improvements, and that pre-announcements almost always lead to disappointment. While acknowledging MT remains good software, he warns that the PR damage threatens Six Apart's credibility, which is essential for any commercial software company.
key_points: Paid upgrades must deliver new features — users won't pay for engine improvements and bug fixes alone, no matter how valuable they are technically
Six Apart's own co-founder admitted MT 3.0 was not a feature release, yet they still tried to charge for it
Pre-announcing MT 3.0 as a 'free upgrade' then launching with restrictive paid licensing destroyed user trust
The best policy is to never announce ship dates, features, or pricing until software is ready to ship
With commercial software, users put their faith in the company — unlike open source where they can fork — so credibility is existential
money_quotes: That's not to say, however, that Six Apart didn't fuck this up, big time.
Everyone loves bug fixes. Everyone loves performance improvements. But what people will pay for are features.
Microsoft is sitting on $50 billion in cash on the basis of this premise.
You can certainly argue that the world would be a better place — or at least filled with better software — if people were willing to pay for non-feature release software. But they're not.
"Under-promise and over-deliver" is good advice for developing a loyal user base. The best way to achieve this, however, is not to promise anything at all.
A company that announces software after it is ready to ship will never be wrong.
The more time you spend talking about future versions of your software, the worse it makes the current version look.
With commercial software, users put their faith in the company behind the product.
key_insight: Charging for upgrades without new features and breaking a public promise of free pricing is a credibility-destroying combination for a commercial software company.
